transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Lily, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi, thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been thinking a lot about some recent experiences, especially this party I went to with Ethan. It was kind of overwhelming, and I just felt this weight of responsibility. I don’t know, it’s hard to explain. I just felt like I should have done something to help him.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that party was a significant experience for you, and it left you feeling overwhelmed and responsible for Ethan. Can you tell me more about what happened that made you feel that way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, Ethan was acting really erratically, and it was just... unsettling. I kept thinking that he was completely out of control, and I felt this urge to fix things, you know? I was anxious the whole time, worried about what might happen next. It felt like I was carrying this heavy burden, and I didn’t know how to handle it. I ended up drinking more than I should have, trying to cope with everything. It was just a lot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like Ethan's behavior really triggered a lot of anxiety for you, and you felt a strong urge to step in and help him, which added to your sense of responsibility. That must have been really tough to manage, especially feeling that weight and then turning to drinking as a way to cope. How did you feel about your own reactions during and after the party?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it was tough. I felt really overwhelmed, like I was in over my head. Looking back, I’m not proud of how I handled it. I mean, drinking didn’t really help anything; it just made me feel more out of control. I guess I felt a bit ashamed, like I should have been stronger or more capable. It’s frustrating because I wanted to be there for Ethan, but I ended up just feeling lost myself. It’s like I was trying to fix things, but I couldn’t even fix my own reactions.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling a mix of frustration and shame about how you handled the situation. Wanting to support Ethan while feeling lost yourself can be really challenging. It’s understandable to feel that way, especially when you were trying to manage your own emotions on top of everything else. What do you think contributes to that feeling of needing to be strong or capable in situations like this?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think part of it comes from wanting to be a good friend. I’ve always felt this pressure to take care of the people I care about, like it’s my responsibility to help them through tough times. I guess I worry that if I can’t do that, then I’m failing somehow. It’s like I have this belief that I should be able to handle everything, and when I can’t, it just makes me feel inadequate. I don’t know if that’s fair to myself, but it’s hard to shake off.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you have a strong sense of responsibility towards your friends, and that desire to be a good friend can create a lot of pressure for you. Feeling inadequate when you can’t meet those expectations is a heavy burden to carry. It’s understandable to want to support those you care about, but it seems like this belief might also lead to a lot of self-criticism. How do you feel when you think about that belief—that you should be able to handle everything?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It’s kind of a mixed bag, honestly. On one hand, I feel like it pushes me to be there for others and to try my best, which can be a good thing. But on the other hand, it’s exhausting. When I think about that belief, I often feel anxious and worried that I’m not doing enough. It’s like I’m constantly measuring myself against this standard that’s really hard to reach. And when I fall short, I just end up feeling really down on myself. It’s frustrating because I want to be supportive, but I also wish I could give myself a break sometimes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that belief creates a real tug-of-war for you—on one side, it motivates you to be supportive, but on the other, it leads to exhaustion and self-criticism. It’s understandable to feel frustrated when you’re holding yourself to such a high standard. Wanting to support others while also needing to care for yourself is a delicate balance. What do you think would help you find that break you’re looking for?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe it would help to remind myself that it’s okay to not have all the answers or to be perfect. I guess I need to learn that it’s okay to take a step back sometimes and focus on my own well-being. Maybe finding ways to cope that don’t involve drinking would be beneficial, too. I’ve thought about talking to someone about all of this, but it’s hard to open up. I just want to feel like I can be there for others without losing myself in the process. It’s a tough balance to strike.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re recognizing the importance of self-compassion and the need to prioritize your own well-being, which is a significant insight. Acknowledging that it’s okay not to have all the answers is a powerful step. It’s also great that you’re considering healthier coping strategies. Opening up can be challenging, but it seems like you’re motivated to find that balance between supporting others and taking care of yourself. What do you think might be a small step you could take toward that balance?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could start by setting some boundaries for myself. Like, if I feel overwhelmed, I could give myself permission to take a break instead of jumping in to fix things right away. I could also try to reach out to a friend or someone I trust to talk about how I’m feeling, just to get it off my chest. It feels a bit daunting, but I think those small steps could help me start to find that balance. I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to prioritize my own needs, too.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Setting boundaries sounds like a really healthy and proactive approach, and it’s great that you’re considering reaching out to someone you trust. Taking those small steps can make a big difference in finding that balance. It’s completely understandable to feel daunted by the idea, but it sounds like you’re already thinking about how to prioritize your own needs, which is a positive shift. How do you feel about taking those steps moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit nervous, honestly. It’s always hard to break out of those old patterns, and I worry about how others might react. But at the same time, I think I’m starting to realize that I can’t keep putting everyone else’s needs before my own. I want to feel more in control and less overwhelmed. So, even though it’s scary, I think I’m ready to try. I just hope I can stick with it and not fall back into my old ways.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely normal to feel nervous about breaking old patterns, especially when it involves setting boundaries with others. It shows a lot of self-awareness that you recognize the need to prioritize your own needs. The desire to feel more in control and less overwhelmed is a strong motivator. It’s okay to take it one step at a time and give yourself grace as you navigate this process. What do you think might help you stay committed to these changes, especially when you feel that pull back to old habits?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might help to remind myself of why I want to make these changes in the first place. Maybe keeping a journal to track my feelings and progress could be useful, so I can see how far I’ve come. I could also lean on my friends for support, letting them know I’m trying to set these boundaries. Having someone to hold me accountable might make it easier to stick with it. I just need to keep reminding myself that it’s okay to prioritize my own well-being and that I don’t have to carry everything on my shoulders.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are really thoughtful strategies! Keeping a journal can be a great way to reflect on your feelings and track your progress, and leaning on your friends for support can help create a sense of accountability. It’s important to remind yourself that prioritizing your well-being is not only okay but necessary. It sounds like you’re building a solid foundation for these changes. How do you feel about sharing your intentions with your friends? Do you think that would be a comfortable step for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think sharing my intentions with my friends could be a good step, but I’m a bit hesitant. I worry about how they might react or if they’ll understand where I’m coming from. But at the same time, I know they care about me, and they might actually be supportive. It could help to have them in my corner as I try to make these changes. I guess I just need to find the right moment to bring it up. It feels a bit vulnerable, but maybe that’s part of the process, too.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel hesitant about sharing your intentions, especially when it feels vulnerable. It sounds like you’re aware of the potential support your friends could offer, which is a positive perspective. Finding the right moment to bring it up can help you feel more comfortable. It’s a brave step to open up, and it seems like you’re recognizing that vulnerability is part of the process of growth. How do you think you might prepare for that conversation when the moment feels right?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could start by thinking about what I want to say beforehand, maybe even writing down some key points. That way, I can express myself clearly without getting too overwhelmed. I might also practice with a friend I trust, just to get more comfortable with the idea. It could help to frame it in a way that explains why I’m making these changes and how I hope they can support me. I want to be honest but also keep it focused on my journey. I think having that preparation could make it feel less daunting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan! Preparing what you want to say and practicing with a trusted friend can definitely help ease some of the anxiety around the conversation. Framing it in a way that focuses on your journey and your needs can also make it clearer for your friends to understand how they can support you. It’s great to see you taking these thoughtful steps to approach this in a way that feels manageable. How do you feel about the idea of practicing with a friend? Do you have someone in mind who you think would be supportive?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I do have a friend in mind—someone who’s always been really understanding and supportive. I think talking it through with them could help me feel more confident. They’ve been there for me in the past, so I trust that they’ll listen without judgment. I just hope I can express everything I want to say without getting too emotional or overwhelmed. But I think having that practice could really help me clarify my thoughts and feel more prepared for when I talk to the others.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful that you have a friend in mind who you trust and who has been supportive in the past. Practicing with someone who listens without judgment can really help you feel more confident and clear about what you want to express. It’s natural to feel a bit emotional when discussing personal topics, but it sounds like you’re preparing yourself well for that conversation. Remember, it’s okay to take your time and express yourself at your own pace. How do you feel about setting a time to practice with your friend?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think setting a time to practice would be really helpful. It gives me something to look forward to and makes it feel more concrete. I’ll probably reach out to them soon and see when they’re available. Just having that plan in place might ease some of the anxiety I’m feeling about the whole thing. I think it’ll be good to get it out there and start working through my thoughts with someone I trust. It feels like a step in the right direction.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a great plan! Having a specific time to practice can definitely help make it feel more concrete and manageable. It’s wonderful that you’re taking this step to work through your thoughts with someone you trust. It seems like you’re moving in a positive direction, and that can be really empowering. As you prepare for this conversation, how do you feel about the progress you’ve made in understanding your feelings and needs so far?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a mix of relief and hope, honestly. Just talking about everything today has helped me clarify a lot of my feelings and the reasons behind them. I’m starting to see that it’s okay to prioritize my own needs and that I don’t have to carry everything alone. It feels good to recognize that I can take steps toward finding that balance. I still have a long way to go, but I think I’m starting to understand myself better, and that feels like a positive shift. I just hope I can keep this momentum going.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s really encouraging to hear that you’re feeling relief and hope, and it’s wonderful that our conversation has helped you clarify your feelings. Recognizing that it’s okay to prioritize your own needs is a significant realization, and it sounds like you’re on a meaningful path toward finding that balance. It’s natural to feel like there’s still more to explore, but acknowledging your progress is an important part of the journey. What do you think might help you maintain that momentum as you continue to navigate these changes?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think staying connected with my supportive friends will be really important. Having that network can help me feel less isolated as I make these changes. I also want to keep journaling to reflect on my feelings and track my progress. It might be helpful to set small, achievable goals for myself, too, so I can celebrate those little victories along the way. I think reminding myself that it’s okay to take things one step at a time will help me maintain that momentum. I just need to keep focusing on my growth and be patient with myself.
